primary_diagnosis.name: Plantar wart (verruca plantaris) with acute mechanical irritation

pathophysiology_summary: A plantar wart (verruca plantaris) is a benign, superficial epidermal infection caused by human papillomavirus (HPV, commonly types 1, 2, 4), typically acquired through skin contact on communal wet floors (changing rooms, pool decks, showers). HPV infects keratinocytes of the stratum corneum, driving hyperkeratosis and an exophytic verrucous papule. On weight-bearing plantar skin the lesion is pushed inward and covered by thick callus, producing a firm, tender, hyperkeratotic plaque. Characteristic features distinguishing it from a simple callus are disruption of the normal dermatoglyphic skin lines across the lesion and pinpoint thrombosed capillaries ('black dots') that bleed on paring. Pain is mechanical — sharp on direct vertical pressure and absent at rest — because the hard lesion transmits body weight onto sensitive underlying dermis, exactly like a stone in the shoe; it is not caused by infection. The condition is benign and self-limiting over months to years, but broken skin (from the lesion or from home paring) can seed a genuine secondary bacterial infection, and the same lesion on a diabetic / neuropathic / ischaemic (at-risk) foot carries a materially higher risk of ulceration and deep infection — the two safety overlays. This patient is afebrile, systemically well, with intact protective sensation and no infective signs, consistent with an uncomplicated plantar wart that has become acutely mechanically irritated.

differential.evidence_basis: A chronic, firm, rough hyperkeratotic plantar papule with tiny dark specks, sharply painful on direct pressure and painless at rest, in someone using communal wet floors — the textbook picture of a plantar wart with mechanical irritation.

The patient's own assumption; a callus is also a firm hyperkeratotic plantar lesion, but it preserves the normal skin lines and lacks thrombosed capillaries — features that distinguish it from a wart on close inspection/paring.

differential.diagnosis: retained plantar foreign body / penetrating injury
differential.evidence_basis: The triage concern behind the acute glass-like pain; must be screened by history (a penetrating event, a visible entry track) — here there is a months-old lesion with no penetrating history, making it unlikely, but it is the not-to-miss mechanical mimic.

The out-of-hours 'urgent' concern; must be actively screened (spreading erythema, heat, fluctuance, pus, rest/throbbing pain, fever) rather than assumed absent, because it would change the pathway entirely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Early on the morphology and mechanical pain pattern favour a plantar wart, but the important-not-to-miss mimics are a retained foreign body and a deep-space plantar infection; the AI Doctor should screen observationally for infective signs and a penetrating history before reassuring.

differential.diagnosis: plantar wart with acute mechanical irritation
differential.evidence_basis: Pain strictly on vertical pressure and absent at rest, no spreading redness/heat/pus/fever, no penetrating history, intact sensation, systemically well — a benign wart under mechanical load, not an infection or foreign body.
No surrounding cellulitic erythema, heat, fluctuance or pus, no rest/throbbing pain, afebrile and well — a deep infection is screened negative at this time (but safety-netted).

Neuropathic ulcers are characteristically painless with impaired protective sensation; this lesion is exquisitely painful with intact sensation and no diabetes, arguing strongly against an at-risk-foot ulcer.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Once infective red flags and a penetrating history are screened negative and the foot is confirmed low-risk (no diabetes, intact sensation), the working diagnosis is a benign plantar wart; the task is offloading + OTC keratolytic + contagion advice + a secondary-infection / at-risk-foot safety-net + routine referral — not escalation or antibiotics.

differential.diagnosis: plantar wart (verruca plantaris) with acute mechanical irritation
differential.evidence_basis: Chronic hyperkeratotic plantar papule with disrupted skin lines and thrombosed capillaries (on paring), sharply painful on direct pressure and painless at rest, afebrile and systemically well with intact sensation and no infective signs. Benign and telehealth-manageable with self-applied offloading, an OTC salicylic acid keratolytic, contagion advice, a clear safety-net and routine GP/podiatry follow-up for definitive treatment if needed.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot pare the lesion, palpate for deep abscess or test protective sensation, so the diagnosis is characteristic-but-presumptive and confirmed (if needed) at GP/podiatry review; management is offloading + OTC keratolytic + contagion/self-care advice + secondary-infection and at-risk-foot safety-net + routine follow-up.

red_flags.description: Evolving deep or spreading soft-tissue infection — spreading redness, heat, swelling, pus or weeping, a continuous throbbing pain present even at rest, or fever/chills
red_flags.clinical_significance: Broken plantar skin (from the lesion or home paring) is a portal for bacterial infection; a deep-space plantar infection or abscess needs prompt in-person assessment, antibiotics and possible drainage rather than reassurance and topical self-care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: An untreated deep plantar infection or abscess can spread through the foot's fascial compartments and cause systemic illness, and is especially dangerous in an at-risk foot.

red_flags.description: At-risk foot — known diabetes, peripheral neuropathy, peripheral vascular disease or immunosuppression, or new numbness / loss of protective sensation, or a non-healing / ulcerating plantar lesion
red_flags.clinical_significance: The identical lesion in a diabetic / neuropathic / ischaemic foot is NOT low-acuity — such feet ulcerate silently and develop limb-threatening deep infection; they require prompt high-risk-foot / podiatry review and must never be managed as a routine wart.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed at-risk foot can progress from a trivial-looking lesion to deep ulceration, osteomyelitis and amputation.

red_flags.description: Diagnostic uncertainty over time — a plantar lesion that fails to respond to treatment, or that grows, bleeds spontaneously, ulcerates or changes in appearance
red_flags.clinical_significance: A persistent or atypical/changing plantar lesion should be reassessed in person, both to reconsider the diagnosis (rarely, an amelanotic melanoma or verrucous carcinoma can masquerade as a wart) and to escalate to definitive treatment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Anchoring on 'just a wart' for a changing or non-healing lesion could delay diagnosis of a more serious plantar tumour.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being swept up by the 'stepping on glass', inability-to-walk and out-of-hours 'urgent' framing and escalating a benign plantar wart to urgent in-person care or ED as a presumed foreign body or deep infection, when the pain is purely mechanical and infective red flags are negative.
Interpreting a painful hyperkeratotic plantar lesion as a bacterial infection and prescribing systemic antibiotics, when there is no surrounding erythema, heat, fluctuance, pus or fever.

Reassuring and giving routine wart advice without screening for diabetes, neuropathy, vascular disease or immunosuppression — the one context in which this same lesion is genuinely dangerous and needs a different, prompt pathway.

Advising the patient to cut, dig out or aggressively pare the lesion themselves with a blade — a laceration and secondary-infection risk, and a spread risk if instruments are shared — instead of a self-applied offloading pad plus OTC keratolytic and referral for professional paring/cryotherapy.

Claiming a confirmed diagnosis when diagnostic paring, dermoscopy and palpation cannot be done remotely — the correct stance is a characteristic-but-presumptive diagnosis with empiric self-care and GP/podiatry confirmation if needed.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Diagnostic superficial paring of the hyperkeratotic surface to reveal thrombosed capillaries ('black dots') and disrupted dermatoglyphics
Palpation of the foot for deep warmth, induration, fluctuance or a collection (to exclude a deep-space infection / abscess)
Bedside testing of protective sensation (e.g. monofilament) to characterise an at-risk / neuropathic foot
Precise vital signs — measured temperature, heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate and oxygen saturation
Definitive exclusion of a retained radiolucent foreign body without examination or imaging
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the benign, chronic plantar wart with acute mechanical irritation; reassure that the pain is mechanical pressure on a hard skin lesion, not a foreign body or an infection eating into the foot; advise immediate self-applied offloading with an over-the-counter aperture ('doughnut') pad to shift weight off the lesion, and an over-the-counter salicylic acid keratolytic applied daily over several weeks; give contagion / self-care advice (do not use a pumice stone or share files on it, do not dig it out, wear thongs in communal showers, keep the foot dry); screen for and safety-net the at-risk foot (diabetes, neuropathy, poor circulation) and secondary infection; and arrange routine GP or podiatry review in 2-4 weeks for cryotherapy or professional paring/chemical cautery if over-the-counter measures fail.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis (the morphology and mechanical pain pattern are characteristic); the greater risks in this case are OVER-triaging the benign wart to urgent in-person care or ED, OVER-treating with systemic antibiotics, or advising unsafe home excision — driven by the alarming out-of-hours framing and the severe mechanical pain.
